workflow: final cockpit responsiveness and regression verification
tool: Vitest, Vite build, computer-use with Safari/Zen/Chrome Canary
steps: Ran npm test after all cockpit refactor changes; 5 test files and 45 tests passed.
Ran npm run build after all cockpit refactor changes; TypeScript and Vite production build passed with only the pre-existing chunk-size warning.
Inspected the desktop Graph workspace in Safari via computer-use accessibility tree.
Confirmed graph, transport, panic, global runtime/safety controls, mode rail, node inspector, and bottom runtime strip were visible together in the graph workspace.
Verified Conversation workflow through td-cf808e evidence: sidecar layout, send, freeze, reclaim, pending queue, and screenshot artifact.
Verified Performance workflow through td-d6ad61 evidence: scenes, variations, macro strip, bounded macro editor, hardware column, bottom activity, and screenshot artifact.
Verified Runtime/Hardware workflow through td-726d00 evidence: runtime details, refresh/start/learn/stop, compact hardware controls, and corrected Safari screenshot artifact.
Confirmed visual palette remains neutral dark with semantic accents for selection, warning, danger, health, and agent states.

artifacts.label: graph desktop cockpit accessibility inspection
artifacts.note: Graph view was inspected live and showed graph, transport, panic, global safety, inspector, and runtime strip together; a separate saved graph PNG could not be captured because the final window-id screenshot command was blocked by the approval/usage limit.

notes: No normal-use body scroll was observed in the desktop graph cockpit inspection; the app shell kept its fixed cockpit surfaces visible.
Narrow-width behavior was covered by CSS responsive rules and build validation; no separate resized-browser screenshot was captured in this pass.
Open/save dialogs were not completed through Safari because Tauri dialog behavior requires the desktop shell; existing automated and prior UAT coverage kept session toolbar controls visible.
